#  Dielectric Properties of Relaxor AFE

The dielectric behavior of the relaxor AFE ceramics, as shown in Fig. 4, raises a critical question: does it truly meet the definition of a "relaxor" or "relaxor ferroelectric"? Classical relaxor ferroelectrics (RFEs) are characterized by a broad, frequency-dispersive dielectric maximum that does not follow the Curie-Weiss law but instead obeys the Vogel-Fulcher (VF) relationship, indicating polar nanoregions (PNRs) with a freezing temperature and critical slowing down of dipole dynamics. In the present system, the temperature-dependent permittivity curves (Fig. 4a) exhibit broadened peaks and diffusive phase transitions, which are reminiscent of relaxor behavior. However, the authors do not provide VF fittings or frequency-dependent dielectric data to confirm whether the system exhibits the characteristic freezing of PNRs. Instead, the observed behavior is attributed to the dynamic nature of HDSs, which are described as highly sensitive to temperature and electric fields. While the free energy profiles in Fig. 4e suggest broadened minima due to heterogeneous energy landscapes, this does not necessarily imply the existence of PNRs or a freezing transition. The absence of evidence for critical slowing down—a hallmark of relaxor dynamics—suggests that the term "relaxor AFE" may be a misnomer. The system appears to exhibit diffusive phase transitions and reduced hysteresis due to structural disorder and local heterogeneity, rather than the cooperative freezing of polar nanoregions. Thus, while the dielectric properties share superficial similarities with relaxors, the underlying mechanism is fundamentally different, and the material is better described as a disordered AFE with heterogeneous dipolar structures rather than a true relaxor.
